title: Replacing a cooker hood bulb and grease filter
subtitle: Swap a failed lamp and a saturated grease filter in an integrated cooker hood
excerpt: How to safely replace a lamp and clean or replace the grease filter in a ceiling-mounted or wall-mounted cooker hood, covering isolation, lamp types, and the difference between washable aluminium mesh filters and replaceable carbon filters.
sourceNotes: IET Guidance Note 1 (selection and erection of equipment). Cooker Hood Association maintenance guidance (open access). Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

glossaryTerms.definition: The filter in a cooker hood that traps airborne fat and grease before it reaches the fan or the ducting. Two types exist: aluminium mesh filters, which are washable in a dishwasher and reusable indefinitely; and charcoal or carbon filters, which are used in recirculating hoods to absorb odours and must be replaced (not washed) every three to six months depending on cooking frequency. The hood type and model determine which filter applies.

content.text: Cooker hoods use several lamp types: G4 halogen capsules, GU10 spots, T8 fluorescent tubes, and in newer hoods, integrated LED modules. Check the label inside the hood or the manual before ordering. A saturated
reduces extraction, raises fire risk, and makes the hood noisier. Replacing or cleaning it alongside the lamp is a sensible combination.

Switch off the hood at its fused spur or isolator, or turn off the MCB at the consumer unit if the hood is plug-in. Confirm the hood is dead with the voltage tester pen before opening the lamp cover or touching any internal component.

Isolate the hood again. Slide or unclip the grease filter from beneath the canopy. For an aluminium mesh filter: wash in the dishwasher on the hottest cycle, or soak in hot water with degreaser for 20 minutes, rinse, and dry fully before refitting. A discoloured filter with visible open mesh is reusable; one that is compressed flat or blocked solid should be replaced. For a carbon filter: slide out the old one and clip in the new. Carbon filters cannot be cleaned.
Step 2, remove and replace the lamp

Open the lamp cover according to the hood type: some have a bayonet glass, some a push-and-twist lens, and some a hinged cover retained by a spring clip. Remove the old lamp: GU10 spots push in and rotate to release; G4 capsules pull straight out from their spring clips; bayonet caps push and turn anti-clockwise. Fit the replacement lamp without touching the glass envelope of halogen capsules with bare fingers (skin oils cause premature failure). Restore power briefly to confirm the new lamp works before refitting the cover.
Step 3, remove and clean or replace the grease filter

Isolate the hood again. Slide or unclip the grease filter from beneath the hood canopy. For an aluminium mesh filter: wash in the dishwasher on the hottest cycle, or soak in hot water with degreaser for 20 minutes, then rinse and allow to dry fully before refitting. A discoloured filter that still has visible open mesh is reusable; one that is compressed flat, blocked solid, or dripping fat should be replaced. For a carbon filter on a recirculating hood: slide out the old filter and clip in the replacement. Carbon filters cannot be cleaned.
Refit the grease filter and the lamp cover. Restore the power and test both the light and the extraction fan on all speed settings.

attrs.symptom: New lamp fails within a few weeks
attrs.cause: The lamp specification does not match the fitting, or a GU10 or G4 capsule was handled with bare fingers and the skin oils caused localised overheating.
attrs.fix: Confirm the replacement lamp matches the original specification exactly. If it is a halogen capsule, clean the glass with a cloth dampened with surgical spirit and fit the replacement wearing gloves or through a paper towel.

attrs.symptom: Hood is still noisy after replacing the grease filter
attrs.cause: The fan blades have accumulated grease and are out of balance, or the carbon filter is blocked and restricting recirculation airflow.
attrs.fix: Check that the carbon filter (if fitted) is the correct type for the hood model and is not beyond its service interval. Clean the fan blades by wiping with a degreaser-dampened cloth, with the hood fully isolated.
